original_abstract: Non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) serves as a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, facilitating the detection and annotation of diverse small molecules within complex environmental matrices. Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) remains the predominant strategy for gathering product ion spectra; however, its effectiveness depends heavily on the optimization of instrument parameters to ensure high spectral quality and broad chemical coverage. In this study, we systematically evaluated the impact of ten key DDA settings on the Q Exactive mass spectrometer using natural organic matter samples sourced from marine, freshwater, and terrestrial environments. Utilizing both classical and feature-based molecular networking via MZmine and GNPS, we assessed performance based on network node density, multivariate clustering, and quality metrics such as annotation rates, MS/MS triggering efficiency, and precursor coverage. Our findings identify *automatic gain control*, *microscans*, *mass resolving power*, and *dynamic exclusion* as the most influential parameters. In contrast, *collision energy*, *TopN*, and *isolation width* exhibited moderate effects, while *apex trigger*, *monoisotopic selection*, and *isotopic exclusion* had minimal impact on the results. These insights into the acquisition dynamics of the Q Exactive platform offer a practical framework for researchers to refine their methods, providing a baseline that may be adaptable to other sample types and mass spectrometry architectures.
humanized_abstract: Data-Dependent Acquisition Parameters Regulating the Outcome of Non-Targeted LC-MS/MS Metabolomics in Natural Organic Matter, Originating from Marine, Freshwater and Terrestrial Environments The identification and characterization of small molecular composition is routinely performed using non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) approach in modern metabolomics studies. Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) as front-end strategy is widely applied, providing product ion spectra that are further used for identification purposes. DDA parameters needs to be well optimized to obtain high quality spectra and as broad as possible chemical information, even when working with complex matrices. Here, we optimized ten DDA parameters on a Q Exactive bench-top high resolution mass spectrometer instrument using natural organic matter samples derived from marine, freshwater and terrestrial origin. All parameters were varied in several times and then using both classical and feature-based molecular networking workflow approaches in MZmine2 and GNPS, metabolomic networks were constructed and evaluated using node density scores of different clusters obtained using multivariate clustering analysis and quality metrics such as number of annotations, occurrence of MS/MS spectra for each precursor ion and corresponding precursor ion coverage. Results showed that the 10 parameters which were varied on the Q Exactive have different impacts on the resulting data, with identification of *automatic gain control*, *microscans*, *mass resolving power*, and *dynamic exclusion* as being the most affecting parameters. Parameters such as *collision energy*, *TopN*, *and isolation width* have somewhat lower effects, while the *apex trigger*, *monoisotopic selection*, and *isotopic exclusion* are less affecting parameters. This study therefore provides experimental conditions which researchers can apply when working with similar samples or when using similar mass spectrometry instrumentation hence could be as a reference data for corresponding applications.
